Fern Por-rrrcAr- PnACTrcrs C oMMrssroN
-128 J Street r Suite 620 o gxg1xnlento. CA 95gl.l-2129
(916) 122-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
October 6,2010
William B. Loscutoff
144 Jalisco Place
Davis, CA 95616
Re
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-10-148
Dear Mr. Loscutoff:
This letter is in response to your request for assistance regarding the "revolving door,,
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Because you are requesting general
information on the operation of the law, and do not inquire about a speciiic transaction, we are
providing you with informal assistance.z Please bear in mind that our advice is based solely on
the provisions of the Act. We can offer no opinion on the application, if any, of such provisions
as Government Code Section 1090, or other post-gouernmeni employment iaws such as public
Contract Code Section_ 10411. We Llrge you to consult with your io.-". agency's counsel or the
Attorney General's office on any possible application of these provisions,-aftei your post-
retirement employment plans take more concrete form.
QUESTTON
You ask first about any restrictions the Act would impose on you following your recent
separation from government employment, if you were to be employeA Uy o company regulated
by your former agency, but in a position involving only clients outside the state of California.
You also ask about employment by another company, where your role would be to facilitate
development and certification by your former agency of that company's new products.
I
rThe Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
ref'erences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair political practices
Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 18997 of Title z of ttre California Code of Regulations. All
regulations may be tirund in Title 2, Division 6 of the Calitbrnia Code of Re-sulations, unless otherwise indicated.
r Intormal assistance cloes not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or turmal
w'ritten advice. (Section 83 I l4l regularion I g329(cx3). copy enclosed).


File No. I-10-148
Page No. 2
The Act does not prevent you from accepting post-govemmental employment in the
private sector, nor from accepting reimbursement from a piospective employer fo, outof-po"f."t
expenses you describe in connection with your travel to Vapoi Systems iecirnologies 1..VbT,';
offices in ohio. You correctly understand that for twelve monthi following your retirement date
you may not make any compensated appearance before your former og"n"f ior the purpose of
influencing administrative or legislative action, or influencing actions or pioceedings, as
described below. Assuming that neither Veeder-Root ("VR"j nor VST was involv.d in ory
judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding during your time at CARB, you would not be suUlect
to the Act's permanent ban.
CONCLUSION
FACTS
r A saf'ety problem with vST- certifled nozzles was discovered six months after yor.rr retirement. As a
result, the State Fire Marshall required modification of the nozzles to remove their "hold open latches,, by october
r5,2010.
Until your retirement on Novemb er 3,2009, you were employed by the California Air
Resources Board ("CARB") as Chief of its Monitoring & Laboratory Oiririon. prior to your
retirement you were responsible for the gasoline marketing vapor recovery program, which
involved developing regulations and test procedures, certifying equipmeni for marketing in
California, and general implementation of California's vapor recovery program. you are now
exploring opportunities to consult with several companies that you ur"d to"r"gulate.
You have been asked to assist one company, VR, with marketing otttside of California.
For VR, you do not expect to have any contact, formal or informal, witlicaRB staff or its board.
However, a second company, VST, has also expressed interest in retaining you as a consultant to
facilitate development and CARB certificationbf future VST produ"tr. friil" not strictly
necessary, your services would be more effective if you *ere able to interact with CARB staff,
including former subordinates.
Prior to your retirernent neither VR nor VST had any judicial, quasi-judicial or other
proceeding pending with CARB. Both companies had been issued Executive Orders (,.EOs,,)
signed by you, certifying their equipment for marketing in California. you believe that these
Eo's have subsequently been superseded by Eos issued to VR and VST by your ru"".rrorJ
You contacted CARB about possibly consulting for VST, and were advised by your
successor' Dr. Ayala, of the likely application of the Act's "one year ban" under Section
87406(dXl), described below, and you agree on its probable appiication in your case. you were
also alerted to the possible application of the Act's ;lifetime ban" and have'requested an opinion
from CARB's legal counsel on that topic. You have not yet received this opinion, and are
following the advice of Dr. Ayala to seek advice from the Commission on thi, topic.


File No. I-10-148
Page No. 3
You have neither signed an agreement nor accepted compensation from either VR or
vST' However' you flew to vST offices in ohio in AJgust to discuss consulting and have been
offered reimbursement for documented travel "^p"rr"r, opproximately $1,000 for airfare, car
rental and three nights in a hotel. To date you have not accepted reimLursement.
Public officials are subject to three types of post-governmental employment restrictions
under the Act. A one-year "revolving door" iestriction bars certain state ernpioyees from
communicating, for compensation, with their former agencies for the purpo* oi influencing
certain administrative or legislative action. (Section SiqO6,Regulation I'g746.I.)
A permanent ("lifetime") ban prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides,,to
participate, for compensation, in a proceeding involving the State of Califomia if the pr:oceeding
is one in which the former state employee participated while employed by the state. (Sections
87 401-87 402, Regulation I 874 l. l.)
Finally, there is a restriction on influencing prospective employment that prohibits a
public employee from participating in, or using hii 
-or 
her official posiiion to influence, a
governmental decision directly relating to any person with whom Le or she is negotiating, or has
an arrangement concerning, prospective employment. (Section 874O7,Regulation 1g747.) your
account of the facts does not suggest that we need to offer advice you on tf,is provision.
The One-Year "Revolving Door, Restriction
Section 87406 prohibits specified public officials from acting as agents or artorneys or
otherwise representin g,for compensation, "any other person, by mafing iny formal or informal
appearance, or by making any oral or written communication, before oriy rtot" administrative
agency' or officer or employee thereof," for one year after the official left the agency,s
employment "if the appearance or communication is made.for the purpose of iifluencing
administrative or legislative action, or influencing any action or proceeding involvtng the
issuance, omendment, awardtng, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, 
"or contract, or the
sale or purchase of goods or property.,' (Emphasis idded.;,
Regulation I 87 46.2(a) furrher provides :
"(a) For purposes of Government code section g7406, a formal or informal
appearance or oral or written communication is for the purpose of influencing if it
is m1!e for the principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing,
modifying, opposing, delaying, or advancing the action oiproceedirg. en
ANALYSIS
r The prohibitions of Section 87.106 do not apply to appearances for which you do not receive
compensation


File No. I-10-148
Page No.4
appearance or communication includes, but is not limited to, conversing by
telephone or in person, corresponding with in writing or by electronic
transmission, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any commllnication.,,
We assume from your employment history that you were required to file a Form 700
"Statement of Economic Interests" as a "designated employee" under CARB's conflict of
interest code, and you are therefore subject to the Act's oni-year ban.
Regulation 18746.1(b) outlines the circumstances when the prohibitions of the one-year
ban will apply. Under this regulation, an official covered by the ori-y"o. ban is prohibited from
making an appearance or communication if all of the following apply:
"(l) The official has left his or her state office or employment, which means he or
she has either permanently left state service or is on i t"uu" of absence.
"(2) The appearance or communication is made within 12 months after leaving
state office or employment.
"(3) The public official is compensated, or promised compensation, for the
appe.uance or communication. However, a payment made for necessary travel,
meals, and accommodations received directly in connection with voluniary
services is not prohibited or limited by this section.
"(4) The appearance or communication is made on behalf of any person as an
agent, attomey, or representative of that person. An appearance or communication
made by a public official solely to represent his or hei personal interests, as
defined inz cal. code Regs., section rg7oz.4, subdiviiion (bXl), is not
prohibited or limited by this section.
"(5) The appearance or communication is made for the purpose of influencing, as
defined in2 CaL Code Regs. Section 18746.2, any legisiative or administrative
action, or any discretionary act involving the issuancJ, amendment, awarding, or
revocation of a permit, license, grant or contract, or the sale or purchase or g:;As
or property.
"(A) Services performed to administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of
an existing permit, license, grant, contract, or sale agreement *oy b" excluded
from the prohibitions of this regulation, provided the services do not involve the
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of any of these actions or
proceedings . . ..
"(6) The appearance or communication is made before any officer or employee of
any of the following:
"(A) Any state administrative agency that the public official worked for or
represented during the 12 months before leaving state office or employment . . ..
"(B) Any state administrative agency which budget, personnel, and otler
op€rations are subject to the direction and control of any agency described in
subdivision (bX6XA) . . ..
"(C) Any state administrative agency subject to the direction and control of the
Governor, if the official was a designated employee of the Governor,s office
during the 12 months before leaving state office or employment.,'


File No. I-10-148
Page No. 5
Communications restricted by the one-year ban include any formal or informal
appearance or oral or written communication made to influence legislative or administrative
action or any action on a proceeding. (Section 87406(dxl).) These communications include,
but are not limited to, conversing directly or by telephone, corresponding by writing or e-mail,
attending a meeting, and delivering orsending any iommunication. @eluiation lg746.2(a).)
A communication is considered to be for the pllrpose of influencing legiiative or administrative
action "if it is made for the principal purpose of iupportirg, pro-oiing, influencing, modifying,
opposing, delaying, or advancing the action or proieedingJ's lRegulition lg746.i(a).)
Certain communications are not restricted under the one-year ban. A communication is
not subject to the one-year ban when the former official:
"(l) Participates as a panelist or formal speaker at a conference or similar public
event for educational purposes or to disseminate research and the subject matter
does not pertain to a specific action or proceeding;
"(2) Attends a general informational meeting, seminar, or similar event;
"(3) Requests information concerning any matter of public record; or
"(4) Communicates with the press." (Regularion rg746.2(b)(l)-(4).)
Thus, the one-year ban would not prevent you from requesting information generally
available to the public about agency business from your former employer or other state agencies.
Nor would it prohibit you from attending informational meetings ..g*aing existing laws,
regulations, or policies, as long as you do not attempt to influeric" t"-girtutire or administrative
actions by your former agency. Social conversations with employeei of your former agency that
are not aimed at influencing its administrative or legislative a"tiors or. not prohibited 6'y tnl Uan.
(Tobias Advice Letter, No. 4-96-0g9.) (Section SZZOOlalt;.;
Whether a particular meeting or conversation is for the purpose of influencing
administrative or legislative action or a specific proceeding (as defined by Regul a[lo; rc746.2)
depends on the facts of each case. For instance, if an ex-employee attends o p"uuti" meeting with
. 
s "Legislative action" is defined at Section 82037 to mean "the drafting, introduction, consideration,
modification, enactment or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other matter by the
Legislature or by either house or any committee, subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof, or by a member or
employee of the Legislature acting in his otficial capacity. ;Legislative action" also means the action of the
Governor in approving or vetoing any bill..' Section tiZOOiful piovides that ..,Administrarive action, means the
proposal, drafting, development, consideration, amendment, enaciment, or defeat by any state agency of any rule,
regulation, or other action in any ratemaking proceeding or any quasi-legislative proceeding, *r,irt shall include any
proceeding governed by Chapter 3.5 (commencing with- Section I t:+o) of part I of Division 3 of Title 2.


File No. I-10-148
Page No. 6
many other persons, where there are many topics on the agenda, it may be reasonable to infer
that the ex-employee's attendance is not for the purpose oi irflu.n"ing the agency's action.
Conversely, where there is a small meeting to discuss a particular administrative or legislative
action, or other specific action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or
revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or propJ.ty
(section 87406(d)), it may more readily be inferred that the former employee's p.esence at the
meeting is intended to influence agency action. (RamirezAdvice Letter, ito. R-gg-:OO.)6
Similarly, as noted above, Regulation 18746.1(b)(5XA) provides that performing services
to administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of an existing permit, licinse, grant,
contract, or sale agreement are not considered appearances or communications p.ohibit"d under
Section 87406, so long as you do not influence other administrative or legislative action, or the
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or
purchase of goods or property
Accordingly, for twelve months after the effective date of your retirement, you may not
make a paid appearance before CARB for the purpose of influencing administrativl or legislative
action, or influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuan-e, amendment, awarding, or
revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or propert!.
This does not prevent you from accepting post-governmental employment iithe private iectlr,
nor does it prohibit you from accepting reimbursement from a prosplctive employer for the out-
of-pocket expenses you describe in connection with your travei to vst officei in ohio.
The Permanent Ban on.,switching Sides,
This permanentbanprohibits a former state employee from "switching sides,,and
participating, for compensation, in a specific proceeding involving the State oT California, or
assisting others in the proceeding, if the proceeding is one in which the former state employee
participated while employed by the state. (See Sections 87401-87402; Regulation ta74t.l.;
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban applicable to any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other
proceeding in which you participated while employed as a state adminiitrative official.
"'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application, request for a
ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation,
arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or partiei in any cotirt or state
administrative agency . . .." (Section 87400(c).) An official has "participated" in a proceeding if
he or she took part in the proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision, opp.oroil
disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering advice orra subsiantial basis,
investigation, or use of confidential information . . .." (Section g7400(d).)
The permanent ban does not apply to a "new" proceeding even in cases where the new
proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior proceeding in wtrich the official had participated.
6 Further information is provided in the enclosed fact sheet prepared by the Commission to address
common questions associated with post-employment restrictions.


File No. I-10-148
Page No. 7
A "new" proceeding nor subject to the permanent ban typically involves different parties, subject
matter, or circumstances that differ from those involved in prwious proceedings. (Risr Advice
Letter' No. A-04-187; also see Donovan Advice Letter, tto. t-O:-t tg.) New cintracts let by the
employee's former agency, in which the former employee did not participate, are considered new
proceedings- (Leslie Advice Letter, No. I-89-649.) A "new" contract is one based on new
consideration and new terms, even if it involves the same parties. (FerberAdvice Letter, No. I-
99-104: Anderson Advice Letter, No. A-98-159.) In addition, the application, drafting, and the
award of a contract, license, or approval is considered a proceeding^separate from theinonitoring
and performance of the contract, license, or approval. (Andersor,-rup)o; BlonienAdvice Letter,-
No. 4-89-463.)
Finally, please bear in mind that Section 87402 prohibits former officials from being paid
to "aid, advise, counsel, consult or assist in representing" any other person in any proceeding in
which the official would be prohibited from appearing under Section g741l. Thus the permanent
ban extends not only to appearances before a former ag"r"y, but to "switching sides,, generally.
Thus it prohibits any form of paid "behind the scenes';consultation or assistaice.
We have outlined the general provisions of the permanent ban simply for your review and
consideration. You state that neither VR nor VST waslnvolved in anyiuiicial, quasi-judicial or
other proceeding during your employment at CARB. Assuming that is ihr.o.", you will not be
subject to any permanent ban under the Act. But absent any inlormation on yourparticipation in
specific proceedings while in state service, and of the relation between any such pioceed-ings and
matters on which you may be employed by a private-sector client, u ,urn*ory of the law is all we
can offer you at present.'
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
J*.,
By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
LTwjgl
Enclosures
We recognize that you have sought advice on this subject from CARB's legal counsel, which may provide
important guidance on any specific "proceedings" that might give rise to a permanent ban.

(section 87406(d)), it may more readily be inferred that the former employee's p.esence at the
meeting is intended to influence agency action. (RamirezAdvice Letter, ito. R-gg-:OO.)6
Similarly, as noted above, Regulation 18746.1(b)(5XA) provides that performing services
to administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of an existing permit, licinse, grant,
contract, or sale agreement are not considered appearances or communications p.ohibit"d under
Section 87406, so long as you do not influence other administrative or legislative action, or the
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or
purchase of goods or property
Accordingly, for twelve months after the effective date of your retirement, you may not
make a paid appearance before CARB for the purpose of influencing administrativl or legislative
action, or influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuan-e, amendment, awarding, or
revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or propert!.
This does not prevent you from accepting post-governmental employment iithe private iectlr,
nor does it prohibit you from accepting reimbursement from a prosplctive employer for the out-
of-pocket expenses you describe in connection with your travei to vst officei in ohio.
The Permanent Ban on.,switching Sides,
This permanentbanprohibits a former state employee from "switching sides,,and
participating, for compensation, in a specific proceeding involving the State oT California, or
assisting others in the proceeding, if the proceeding is one in which the former state employee
participated while employed by the state. (See Sections 87401-87402; Regulation ta74t.l.;
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban applicable to any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other
proceeding in which you participated while employed as a state adminiitrative official.
"'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application, request for a
ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation,
arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or partiei in any cotirt or state
administrative agency . . .." (Section 87400(c).) An official has "participated" in a proceeding if
he or she took part in the proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision, opp.oroil
disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering advice orra subsiantial basis,
investigation, or use of confidential information . . .." (Section g7400(d).)
The permanent ban does not apply to a "new" proceeding even in cases where the new
proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior proceeding in wtrich the official had participated.
6 Further information is provided in the enclosed fact sheet prepared by the Commission to address
common questions associated with post-employment restrictions.
A "new" proceeding nor subject to the permanent ban typically involves different parties, subject
matter, or circumstances that differ from those involved in prwious proceedings. (Risr Advice
Letter' No. A-04-187; also see Donovan Advice Letter, tto. t-O:-t tg.) New cintracts let by the
employee's former agency, in which the former employee did not participate, are considered new
proceedings- (Leslie Advice Letter, No. I-89-649.) A "new" contract is one based on new
consideration and new terms, even if it involves the same parties. (FerberAdvice Letter, No. I-
99-104: Anderson Advice Letter, No. A-98-159.) In addition, the application, drafting, and the
award of a contract, license, or approval is considered a proceeding^separate from theinonitoring
and performance of the contract, license, or approval. (Andersor,-rup)o; BlonienAdvice Letter,-
No. 4-89-463.)
Finally, please bear in mind that Section 87402 prohibits former officials from being paid
to "aid, advise, counsel, consult or assist in representing" any other person in any proceeding in
which the official would be prohibited from appearing under Section g741l. Thus the permanent
ban extends not only to appearances before a former ag"r"y, but to "switching sides,, generally.
Thus it prohibits any form of paid "behind the scenes';consultation or assistaice.
We have outlined the general provisions of the permanent ban simply for your review and
consideration. You state that neither VR nor VST waslnvolved in anyiuiicial, quasi-judicial or
other proceeding during your employment at CARB. Assuming that is ihr.o.", you will not be
subject to any permanent ban under the Act. But absent any inlormation on yourparticipation in
specific proceedings while in state service, and of the relation between any such pioceed-ings and
matters on which you may be employed by a private-sector client, u ,urn*ory of the law is all we
can offer you at present.'
